taskVersion.name: epu_q2_medium
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_medium")
def epu_q2_medium(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, medium_pre, medium_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

TARGET ARTICLE START
NEW ORLEANS - Lightweights they're not: Five lawyers, two sons of former mayors, several legislators and one recent MBA graduate are among the political figures in the New Orleans mayor's race.But the impressive array of talent qualifying for the race has a down side: With seven well-known men running for mayor, somebody ltoks to get embarrassed on Feb. 5.Still, the threat that political obituaries may be written hasn't dampened the temptation to enter the race to succeed Sidney Barthelemy, who's not seeking re-election. Nor even has the host of problems that awaits the winner once the victory celebration ends.Those problems include rampant crime, budget deficits and dealing for the first time with the potentially overwhelming influence of the new gambling industry.In addition, voters have lost confidence in the Police Department (it is virtually a given that the department will be thoroughly shaken up, probably with a new chief) and they are hostile toward politicians who don't seem to share their disgust with City Hall's generally ptor performance."It's going to take some courage to be able to come to grips with these real problems," said Joseph Giarrusso, an at-large councilman and former police chief who is retiring from city politics."People are going to have to see something visible. They don't want to see a lot of plans and a lot of commissions formed."The next mayor also faces anuncertainrelationship with the City Council, which, thanks to a new term-limit rule, will have a majority of new members.Last week the field finally set itself:* Council member Lambert Boissiere Jr., with his new Tulane isniversity MBA degree to polish his business credentials.* 1st District Assessor Ken Carter, a product of the Central City BOLD organization.* Rep. Sherman Copelin Jr., the House speaker pro tem, whose business activities often land him in controversy.* Rep. Mitch Landrieu and Sen. Marc Morial, both sons of memorable New Orleans mayors.* Former mayoral candidate Donald Mintz, who is pushing the same reform agenda he did four years ago and,* Lawyer Roy Raspanti, who grabbed attention with huge campaign signs and steady forum appearances.The 10-man field also includes three unknowns: Democrats Arthur P. Jacobs and Julius "Chip" Leahman and Jerome E. Slade, who filed as an independent.Although Mr. Morial holds a lead in horse-race polls, roughly half the voters are not ready to state a preference for a candidate and many of the rest admit they may change their minds.Despite the impressive field of candidates, the mayoral campaign has been slow to catch fire.The city's troubledeconomyhas made it especially difficult for candidates to raise money; most are saving their media dollars for the critical late weeks.
TARGET ARTICLE END
